message.group-title: Intensive Care and Critical Care Medicine
message.abstract:
<jats:title>ABSTRACT</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Introduction</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>The COVID-19 pandemic required a careful management of intensive care unit (ICU) admissions, to reduce ICU overload while facing resources’ limitations. We implemented standardized, physiology-based, ICU admission criteria and analyzed the mortality rate of patients refused from the ICU.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Materials and Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    COVID-19 patients proposed for ICU admission were consecutively analyzed; Do-not-resuscitate patients were excluded. Patients presenting a SpO
                    <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                    lower than 85% and/or dyspnea and/or mental confusion resulted eligible for ICU admission; patients not presenting these criteria remained in the ward with an intensive monitoring protocol. Primary outcome was both groups’ survival rate. Secondary outcome was a sub analysis correlating SpO
                    <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                    cutoff with ICU admission.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    From March 2020 to January 2021, 1623 patients were admitted to our Center; 208 DNR patients were excluded; 97 patients underwent intensivist evaluation. The
                    <jats:italic>ICU-admitted</jats:italic>
                    group mortality rate resulted 15.9% at 28 days and 27% at 40 days; the
                    <jats:italic>ICU-refused</jats:italic>
                    group mortality rate resulted 0% at both intervals (p < 0.001). With a SpO
                    <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                    cut-off of 92%, the hypoxia rate distribution did not correlate with ICU admission (p = 0.26); with a SpO
                    <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                    cut-off of 85%, a correlation was found (p = 0.009). A similar correlation was also found with dyspnea (p =0.0002).
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusion</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    In COVID-19 patients, standardized ICU admission criteria appeared to reduce safely ICU overload. In the absence of dyspnea and/or confusion, a SpO
                    <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                    cutoff up to 85% for ICU admission was not burdened by negative outcomes. In a pandemic context, the SpO
                    <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                    cutoff of 92%, as a threshold for ICU admission, needs critical re-evaluation.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Admission criteria in critically ill COVID-19 patients: a physiology-based approach
